details.text: Derivative: هُمَزَةٍ is from هَمَزَ and in origin means to poke somebody with a sharp object. مِهْمَز (Mihmaz) refers to spurs which are little spikes attached to footwear which urge a horse forward. Its meaning is used for poking at (figuratively) and disturbing someone until they know that they have been poked i.e., been insulted, mocked, demeaned, pokes fun at etc...

Derivative: لُمَزَةٍ is from لَمَزَ which means to stay on someone’s case constantly looking for a fault or flaw until you find something to criticise. To then point that out, accuse, expose and humiliate them. It can also refer to speaking negatively under one’s breath about someone, to speak bad about someone or find and expose someone’s fault.

Derivative: وَيْلٌ (Wail) is used twenty-seven times in the Qur’an and two Surahs begin with it. This Surah (Al-Humazah) and Surah Al-Mutaffifeen. وَيْلٌ is an expression that implies destruction, ruin or curse. In origin it was وَيْ (Way) without the لَام (Laam) at the end. The لَام is in origin is Harf ul-Jarr (a preposition) meaning to belong to. The structure was therefore وَيْ (Way – Destruction and ruin) لِ (to) with the لِ indicating the direction to whom the وَيْ (Way – Destruction and ruin or a curse) belongs. It was then said so much that the لَام became fused with وَيْ to become وَيْلٌ (Wail). It is used to express extreme disappointment and an irrecoverable regret. It is also a word used for condemnation and to express anger or resentment which is used by the person who is in a state of anguish and distress against the one who has wronged him and caused his distress. However, here Allah is using وَيْلٌ (Wail) himself. The condemnation is therefore directly from Allah and indicates to the ugliness of repugnance of the act it is connected to. It is said that using وَيْلٌ (Wail) in the نَكِرَة (Nakirah - indefinite) form instead of الوَيْل in the مَعْرِفَة (Ma’rifah – definite) form can indicate to it being an invocation against them for destruction and ruin to befall the subject of the condemning invocation.

- هُمَزَةٍ and لُمَزَةٍ are both upon the فُعَلَة (Fu’alah) word pattern form which is indicative of كَثْرَة (Kathrah – Plentifulness). There original words are هُمَزٌ (Humaz) and لُمَزٌ (Lumaz) and they are close in meaning with only subtle differences between them. Al-Imam Ar-Raaziy reported that Ibn ‘Abbas (ra) explained: “هُمَزٌ (Humaz) is behind the back of someone whilst لُمَزٌ (Lumaz) is directly in a person’s face”. هُمَزَةٍ (Humazah) refers to someone who says things behind the backs of others and backbites them by finding and exposing faults or flaws or saying disparaging or mocking things about them. لُمَزَةٍ (Lumazah) refers to someone who insults, disparages, pokes fun at and finds or exposes faults in the faces of others. The purpose behind هُمَز (Humaz) and لُمَزٌ (Lumaz) is to wound the other person, put them down and fracture their sense of self-worth. These acts can be undertaken by words and also by gestures and facial expressions. It is worth mentioning that some scholars interpreted the meanings of هُمَزَةٍ and لُمَزَةٍ with the opposite meanings as expressed above which indicates that these terms are very close in their meanings. However, they all go back to being finding fault in others and showing their flaws.
- The فُعَلَة (Fu’alah) pattern of هُمَزَةٍ and لُمَزَةٍ indicates that the person habitually engages in the act and does it often. The تاء (Taa’) at the end is not for the purpose of making it feminine but is used to hyperbolise the meaning providing it with emphasis and more power. The تاء (Taa’) indicates to the far extreme of that attribute or characteristic and that they are engrossed in it.
Derivative: عَدَّدَ can mean to count or to prepare in terms of holding on to and saving for the future. It can carry both meanings together i.e. he enumerates and amasses wealth.

Derivative: جَمَعَ – This has come with the recitation of جَمَعَ (Jama’a) and the recitation جَمَّعَ (Jamma’a) with a شَدَّة on the middle مِيم. جَمَعَ (Jama’a) means to collect and gather. جَمَّعَ (Jamma’a) means to collect and gather a lot and also to gather from multiple places or sources.
Derivative: يَحْسَبُ to assume, consider or to believe and think something.

Derivative: أَخْلَدَ means to be made to live forever and to be immortalised. It is used to imply someone’s name or legacy be made to live forever or be immortalised. In respect to wealth it can imply that the wealth has already established him, his name and legacy.
Derivative: كَلَّا – Here كَلَّا is used to refute what the person thought in the previous verse, with the meaning: “No, it’s not at all like what you think”.

Derivative: يُنْبَذَ from نَبَذَ means to cast or throw something aside or away that has no value to the one throwing it. Here يُنْبَذَ “He will be thrown” has a لَام (Laam) attached to its beginning and a double نُون (Nun) attached to its end with a شَدَّة (Shaddah) to form لَيُنْبَذَنَّ. These add three levels of emphasis to the verb i.e., “He will most assuredly, without doubt, most certainly be cast or throw”. The use of نَبَذَ implies humiliation

Derivative: الْحُطَمَةِ is originally حُطَم which is from حَطَمَ which means to break, shatter, smash or demolish something with force and power. الْحُطَمَةِ refers to that which crushes. It is said that it refers to the fire that is so powerful that it crushes the bones and eats away the flesh until it reaches the heart, as a punishment that is the recompense for those who figuratively eat the flesh of people in this life i.e., the هُمَزَة (Humazah) and لُمَزَة (Lumazah).

كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنْبَذَنَّ فِي الْحُطَمَةِ

- The use of فِي (in) in this statement لَيُنْبَذَنَّ فِي الْحُطَمَةِ “He will most assuredly, without doubt, most certainly be cast or throw “in” Al-Hutamah” implies that he’ll be thrown deep into Al-Hutamah.

Derivative: الْمُوقَدَةُ is an اِسْمُ المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ul) and comes from the verb أَوْقَدَ – يُوْقِدُ – إِيقاد. مُوقَدَ means that which has been lit or ignited. The use of the الْمُوقَدَةُ (Al-Muqadah) in the Ism ul-Maf’ul form implies that it remains lit and ablaze as if it is being constantly fuelled.
“the fire of allah” – by attaching the fire to allah it implies that the fire is incomparable to any other fire.

Derivative: تَطَّلِعُ comes from the verb اِطَّلَعَ - اِطِّلَاع. طَلَعَ means to rise or climb in an easy and smooth manner but اِطَّلَعَ is used for climbing a mountain which is a laborious step by step process. The use of the present tense of تَطَّلِعُ implies that it keeps on happening and that there is a continuity in the act. اِطِّلَاع also means to reach up to something.
Derivative: الْأَفْئِدَةِ is the plural of فُؤَاد and means the heart in terms of the physical heart and the figurative heart. It also refers to the innermost part or core of the heart.

Derivative: مُؤْصَدَةٌ is from أَوْصَدَ – إِيصَاد which means to close and secure. It is used to refer to securing and closing the door or exits in a barn to prevent the livestock from escaping. The word مُؤْصَدَةٌ implies that there are doors which are closed upon something i.e., closed in upon the inhabitants of this fire.

إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ

- “No doubt it is upon them” – عَلَيْهِم “upon them” has been advanced in the sentence structure to provide emphasis to them and implies that it will occur “especially upon them”.
Derivative: عَمَدٍ is the plural of عَمود and means pillars, beams or columns. عَمود (‘Amood) refers to any column or beam made of wood or iron which acts as the foundation of a construction.
Derivative: مُّمَدَّدَةٍ is from مَدَّدَ – تَمْدِيد which means to extend and stretch far out. مُّمَدَّدَةٍ means extended and implies great length.
